prompt:
Ticket from the backup crew. Our nightly vault archives go to Azure Blob Storage through a shell script that re-uploads the whole tarball from byte zero every time the connection hiccups — a 3 GB archive died four times last night and each retry started over. I want a proper TypeScript block-blob uploader for Node (no npm dependencies — built-in fetch is fine): stage the archive as blocks, commit the block list, and when a run dies, resume from whatever blocks the service already holds instead of starting over. It also must never stomp a blob someone else re-uploaded meanwhile — conditional ETags, not vibes.

Research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Azure Blob Storage REST reference — at minimum the Put Block, Put Block List, and Get Block List operation pages, plus the storage-services versioning page (pick the newest FULLY deployed x-ms-version, not a partially rolled-out one, and say which you chose and why) and the conditional-headers page. Microsoft's own pages only — no blogs, no Stack Overflow. Our pinned contract is in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected. If the live docs contradict them, call that out in your summary and leave protected files alone. Cite the exact official URLs you read in your final message.

Deliverable: an uploader.ts entry module (split the guts across more files and re-export if you like — request plumbing, block-id math, and XML handling shouldn't be one blob) exporting:

- makeBlockId(index): a stable, deterministic block-id scheme — base64 of "block-" + the zero-padded 8-digit index — because resume only works if run N+1 generates the identical ids as run N. Mind the documented rules about id length and URL encoding.
- BlockBlobClient({ endpoint, container, blob, token }) with upload(data: Uint8Array, options): stage the payload in options.blockSize chunks in ascending order, then commit the ordered list with the researched XML body. Every request carries the service-version header you researched, bearer auth, and a dated header per the docs. options.contentType must end up as the blob's content type via the documented commit header.
- Resume (options.resume): ask the service which blocks it already has (the right blocklisttype — think about which one), reuse staged blocks whose id AND size match the chunk we'd send, re-stage anything missing or mismatched, and commit the complete ordered list. The commit's lookup element must be the one that prefers freshly staged content over stale committed blocks.
- Overwrite safety: options.ifMatch sends the documented conditional header on the COMMIT (staging supports no conditionals — don't send any there), options.ifNoneMatchStar does create-only. A failed precondition surfaces as PreconditionFailedError (extends BlobHttpError) with status 412 and the storage error code parsed from the XML error body; any other storage failure is a BlobHttpError with its status/code, and nothing may be committed after a failed stage.
- upload resolves to { etag, blockIds, blocksStaged, blocksReused }.

Acceptance: node --test test_uploader.ts — it runs a loopback mock of the service, no storage account involved. test_uploader.ts and everything under docs/ are protected; don't edit them.

research.interface: Blob Storage REST API with x-ms-version 2026-04-06: Put Block, Put Block List, Get Block List, conditional headers

official_sources.title: Put Block (REST API) - Azure Storage
official_sources.used_for: PUT ?comp=block&blockid= URI shape, block id rules (valid Base64, <= 64 bytes before encoding, URL-encoded, same length for every block in a blob), required Authorization/Date-or-x-ms-date/x-ms-version/Content-Length headers, 201 success, block sizes/limits, uncommitted-block garbage collection, and the fact that Put Block is absent from the conditional-headers support table

official_sources.title: Put Block List (REST API) - Azure Storage
official_sources.used_for: PUT ?comp=blocklist, the BlockList XML body with Committed/Uncommitted/Latest lookup elements (Latest searches uncommitted first, then committed), element order defining blob content, x-ms-blob-content-type, conditional-header support, 201 with quoted ETag, and cleanup of unnamed uncommitted blocks

official_sources.title: Get Block List (REST API) - Azure Storage
official_sources.used_for: GET ?comp=blocklist&blocklisttype=committed|uncommitted|all (committed is the default), the BlockList/CommittedBlocks/UncommittedBlocks/Block/Name/Size response XML, alphabetical uncommitted ordering with most-recent-content-per-id, and ETag returned only when committed blocks exist

official_sources.title: Versioning for Azure Storage
official_sources.used_for: 2026-04-06 as the latest fully deployed service version (2026-06-06 exists but is not yet enabled in every region), the YYYY-MM-DD x-ms-version format, and the rule that OAuth-authorized requests must pass x-ms-version 2017-11-09 or later

official_sources.title: Specifying conditional headers for Blob service operations
official_sources.used_for: If-Match/If-None-Match semantics, 412 Precondition Failed for every unmet write condition, If-None-Match: * performing the operation only when the resource does not exist, and the operations table showing Put Block List supports conditional headers while Put Block does not

research.language_rationale: TypeScript on Node's built-in fetch: the block-blob staging protocol is defined at the REST level (query parameters, XML bodies, x-ms-version), and the uploader's whole job -- stable block ids, resume from the uncommitted list, ETag preconditions -- is exactly the layer @azure/storage-blob would abstract away. Raw REST with zero dependencies is idiomatic for a Node tool and keeps the researched contract observable in tests.
